Electronic signatures and digital signatures are both ways of signing a document electronically, but they work in slightly different ways.

An electronic signature is any electronic symbol, sound, or process attached to or logically associated with a document and executed or adopted by a person with the intent to sign the document. This can include a typed name, a scanned image of a handwritten signature, or even just a checkmark to indicate agreement.

A digital signature, on the other hand, uses encryption to securely sign a document. It requires the use of a digital certificate, which is issued by a third party and verifies the identity of the person signing the document. A digital signature is more secure than an electronic signature because it is much harder to forge.

In summary, an electronic signature is a simple way to sign a document electronically, while a digital signature is a more secure way to do so. Both can be used to become paperless and sign documents electronically, but a digital signature is generally considered more reliable and secure.

It is generally not a good idea to try to escape a bear by climbing a tree. Bears are excellent climbers and may be able to follow you up the tree. In addition, some bears, such as black bears, are more likely to leave you alone if you remain calm and try to make yourself look larger by holding your arms out or using a jacket or blanket to appear bigger. 
If you are being chased by a bear, the best thing to do is to try to get away from the bear as quickly as possible by running in a straight line. Do not run up a hill or try to climb a tree. Instead, try to put something between you and the bear, such as a car or a building, and make a lot of noise to try to scare the bear away. 
If the bear is attacking you, try to protect your head and neck with your arms and fight back aggressively with any object that you can find, such as a stick or a rock.

H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide) is poisonous because it is a strong oxidizer. This means that it can easily react with other substances and cause them to break down, which can be harmful if it occurs inside your body. On the other hand, H2O (water) is not poisonous. In fact, it is essential for life and is found in every cell in the human body. The difference between H2O and H2O2 is that extra oxygen atom. This extra atom makes H2O2 much more reactive than H2O, which is why it can be harmful if ingested. It's a bit like a match and a stick of dynamite. Both are made of wood, but the dynamite has something extra (in this case, a chemical explosive) that makes it much more dangerous. Similarly, H2O2 has that extra oxygen atom that makes it more reactive and potentially harmful.

The first thing I know of is that atmospheric friction with space would slow our planet 's orbit causing us to slowly spiral inward toward the sun ( probably in a very short time ) . This would have the unfortunate effect of destroying the planet . Other problems would be heat would create convective winds around the sun . The winds would approach thousands - maybe millions - of miles per hour which would wreak havoc on our own atmosphere . Depending on the composition of the air , it could have tremendous effects on our atmosphere - chemically things could bond and create poisonous gasses or what not . Space flight might not be as difficult , but it would have new problems . We could use the air as a fuel source ( possibly like airplanes use our atmosphere as half of their fuel source , the O2 ) , but we could n't achieve as fast speeds because the friction with the air would heat the spaceship and burn it up if we go too fast . Therefore , we 'd be limited to a few mach ( probably ~ Mach 5 , or about 3500 miles per hour ) . That the slower speeds it would take 81 days to reach the moon ( instead of the 3 days it takes now ) and to mars ... it takes about 9670 days , or 27 years ! Other effects might include things like gravity causing the air to slowly condense back in on itself so the universe would just re - collapse into a ball , ignite like a star , blow up and leave a big black hole . Gasses around black holes would be accelerated high enough to create constant outpourings of x - rays and gamma rays . Ripples through the air caused by shockwaves from super novae and just stars burning would be constantly hitting Earth , bouncing us around and being really noisy ( probably deafening ) . Basically , the solar system could n't work with air instead of a vacuum .

The U.K. signed on because they were totally isolated in Europe . They almost came to blows with both France and Russia and sought an understanding to protect their far - flung empire . Italy was an ally of Germany and Austria , but changed their mind because they wanted Dalmatia ( Austrian territory ) . The Allies could offer them a better deal . Germany eventually did n't have any major allies other than Austria , which they had long historical ties with . They gave Austria a blank check because otherwise they 'd be totally isolated and surrounded by enemies . The Ottoman Empire allies with Germany because every other major European power was looking to carve up their remaining territory . Russia especially desired their capital , Constantinople , which was the ancestral home of the Orthodox Church , controlled the trade through the Black Sea , and was the New Rome . The czar ( derived from " Caesar " ) saw Russia as the Third Rome and dreamed of conquering the ancient Byzantine capital .

There are two different principles at work and they should * not * be confused : 1 ) the speed at which the * electric field * is set up 2 ) the * drift velocity * of electrons in a conductive medium under which an electric field has been established . Electric field is the * potential field * that applies force on electrons and induces them to move . It 's * not * electricity . Similar to magnetism , its a force field set up by an electric charge . When you rub your hair on the carpet and your hair stands up on end , the electric field is forcing hairs to do this , though nothing is moving . An electric field is established at the speed of light in that medium . It applies force on charged particles ( like your hair or an electron ) . An electron is a charged particle . Apply an electric field near it and it will want to move . When an electron moves ( or when * many * move together ) we get a current . Electrons move through a conductor when an electric field is established in the conductor . The velocity of the electrons is the drift velocity . It 's very slow . [ Wikipedia ] ( URL_0 ) does a typical example that shows 1m / hour . But this does n't mean the * signal * takes 1m / hr . Free moving electrons permeate a conductor akin to cars on a train . They 're everywhere . And when the first car moves , so does the last . So if I push the last train car 1 m , the first car ( maybe 1 km away ) moves 1 m instantaneously and the guy at the end of the train has received the signal .

The first IBM PC was powered by an 8088 CPU . That was quickly changed to an 8086 CPU ( the 8086 part was slightly less expensive and basically delivered the same features ) , and all the successful PC clones used that architecture as intel evolved it - to the 80286 , 80386 and 80486 . intel lost a trademark battle over " 486 " and thereafter they started using names they could trademark like Pentium . But the chips in that family still used the underlying instructions that started in the 8088/8086 . If you look at the folders in a vanilla 64 bit Windows install you will see two folders - " Programs " and " Programs ( x86 ) " . Microsoft wanted developers to put applications that used the 32 - bit architecture of the x86 processors in the " Programs ( x86 ) " folder , and 64 bit applications in the " Programs " folder . Microsoft is not good at naming things for clarity .

And worst of all , Microsoft essentially seemed to believe MSIE was * done * at that point . There was a 5 year break between MSIE 6 and MSIE 7 . MS essentially believed that since they had the biggest market share of all browsers , they would n't * need * to improve the browser . Surprise surprise : Firefox and Safari started kicking MSIE 's butt and eating their user base * really * badly . Microsoft woke up and cried " holy crap " and actually started to modernise their shit , fix all the bugs , and make a much better stab at following the standards . And now Microsoft has Chrome to worry about , too .

Baking foods have a set temperature for a reason . If a food wants you to cook it at 400^o F then cooking it at 350 wo n't be as good . Putting the food in right when you turn it on is like cooking it at a lower temperature . Different oven temps cook in different ways mostly because of water . Let 's look at some different temperatures and how they cook . * * 250^o F * * Very low temperatures like 250 are not hot enough to brown the stuff . They will cook very slowly so the thing will usually need to be in there an hour or more . You probably do n't need to pre heat because it takes so long anyway . * * 325ish * * A middle temperature like 325 could brown the stuff but probably wo n't for while . Water usually stops food from being browned because the evaporating water cools it down . If the temperature is around 325 then the water evaporating does n't happen fast enough to dry out the outside of the food so it does n't brown much or only browns slowly . Sometimes it sort of dries out a lot of the food but never enough to brown . * * 400 * * 400 + is good at browning food because it can heat the water out of the outside of the food . Once the outer layer is sort of dry it can brown . TL;DR : If you are too lazy to wait then you do n't have to . It wo n't come out as good because it wo n't brown right . It may come out sort of dry on the outside but not crispy . If you do n't care about that , go for it .

A 401k is an account where you can contribute money before it is taxed , however you can only access it when you legally retire ( there are exceptions I will cover ) . So why is this such a benefit ? Well , as you know , your salary is taxed . The amount is fairly substantial regardless of what tax bracket you 're in . Let 's just say it 's 30 % to use a round number . So what a 401k does is allow you to take a portion of your income ( either a % or certain amount ) and put it in this account before it gets taxed at 30 % . This amount can then earn interest and grow ( depending what it is invested in ) , and then when you retire you receive the amounts in your account back , though you have to pay taxes on it at that point . So if you have to pay taxes on it at the end anyway , why is it so good ? Well , over the years that it is in the 401k , it is earning a return on the full untaxed amount , as opposed to you taking savings ( which have already been taxed 30 % ) and keeping it in a bank account that way . The Loudness War is defined as a trend in recorded music that has gotten generally louder and louder over time . Early records were relatively quiet ; later records would record at louder volumes ; compact discs set a digital limit that sound engineers would come up against through different compression techniques , and so on . As time goes on , it seems the louder music wins out as being the more popular music of the day . Many consumers do n't seem to care as much for sound quality as they do for the loudness of the music , as that can grab their attention . As for what side you 're on ? Well , that 's a personal question . Do you prefer more dynamic sound with good quality , or is it all Top 40 hits at max volume for you ?

When you lock your kneecaps, you are preventing your legs from bending. This can cause your muscles to tense up and become fatigued, which can lead to feeling lightheaded or dizzy. When you stand for a long time with your knees locked, you may not be able to get enough blood flow to your brain, which can cause you to feel faint or pass out. This is because the muscles in your legs help to pump blood back up to your heart and brain. When they are tense and not moving, it can be harder for the blood to circulate properly.

To avoid this, it is important to take breaks and stretch your legs when standing for long periods of time. It is also helpful to keep your knees slightly bent, which can help to improve blood flow and reduce muscle fatigue. By taking these precautions, you can help to prevent fainting or passing out due to locking your kneecaps.

While it 's absolutely correct to call it " debt , " if you have n't studied economics carefully it 's really easy to misunderstand what that means . Imagine you 're starting a new country from scratch , out on some desert island or something . We 'll assume for sake of brevity that you already understand why you need money , so the question becomes just how to make some . The most obvious thing to do is just * declare * that some money exists , using your absolute power as self - appointed king , or something . But we know from history that this is n't the best way to go about it , for a variety of reasons I 'll skip over . So instead , you do what history tells you is the right thing : You establish , using your absolute power as king , two institutions . One is a part of the government , and it 's called the Treasury . The Treasury has the power to create and sell things called * bonds . * ( I 'll explain what those are in a second . ) The other institution you create is called the * central bank . * The central bank has the power to create money and use it to buy bonds . To make some money , you order your Treasury to create a bond and offer it for sale . The central bank buys that bond with money that it just * created out of thin air . * The bond goes to the central bank , the money goes to the Treasury , and you ( as king ) can use that money to buy an aircraft carrier , or whatever . A bond is * technically * a type of debt . By creating that bond and selling it to the central bank , the Treasury * technically * borrowed that money . But I use the word " technically " here to emphasize that we are n't talking about household debt or household borrowing . At your level and mine , borrowing works in a specific kind of way : we borrow money today , then have to pay it back tomorrow , and if we ca n't , we get into trouble . At the level of monetary policy , however , the system of bond sales from the Treasury to the central bank is * how money is created . * If not for creating that bond and selling it to the central bank , there would be no money in your economy . Creating and selling sovereign bonds is how countries create money to start with . So no , there 's never going to be an " end to the payments , " because at the level we 're talking about , it simply does n't work like that . The national debt of the United States is the reason why US dollars exist . If the national debt of the United States were ever fully wiped out — if every bond ever sold by the US Treasury were ever bought back — dollars would * cease to exist , * because they 'd all have made their way back to the place where they originally came from .

Planets are held in orbit around their star by the balance between the star's gravitational pull and the planet's own momentum. Imagine you are on a playground, swinging on a swing. As you swing back and forth, you are pulling against the Earth's gravitational pull with your own momentum. The Earth's gravity is what is keeping you from flying off into space, but your own momentum is what is keeping you from crashing into the ground.

The same thing happens with planets orbiting their star. The star's gravity is pulling the planet towards it, but the planet is also moving really fast in its orbit. This combination of gravity and momentum keeps the planet in a stable orbit around the star, rather than crashing into it.

Even though the star's gravity is constantly pulling the planet towards it, the planet is able to maintain its distance from the star because of its momentum. As long as the planet has enough momentum, it will continue to orbit the star rather than being pulled into it.

There are three naturally occurring isotopes of carbon : carbon-12 ( C-12 ) , carbon-13 ( C-13 ) , and carbon-14 ( C-14 ) . * C-12 has 6 protons , 6 neutrons , and 6 electrons * C-13 has 6 protons , 7 neutrons , and 6 electrons * C-14 has 6 protons , 8 neutrons , and 6 electrons These three isotopes occur in known proportions relative to each other , which means , about 98.89 % of all carbon on Earth is C-12 , 1.1 % is C-13 , and about 0.0000000001 % is C-14 . C-12 and C-13 are stable , which means they do n't spontaneously decay into other elements . C-14 however , is unstable and has a half - life of 5,730±40 years . That means that if I had 1 gram of C-14 right now , in 5,730 years , give or take 40 years , only half a gram would remain , while the other half gram would have turned into something else ( Nitrogen-14 , actually ) . In another 5,730 years , give or take 40 years , I 'd only have a quarter of a gram of C-14 . How does this tell us the age of , say a fossilized animal ? During the animal 's lifetime it would have eaten carbon - containing plants and animals , and incorporated that carbon into its own tissues . What 's important to remember is that it would have absorbed C-12 , C-13 , C-14 in their naturally occurring ratios . So , while it was alive , the carbon in its body would have been 98.89 % C-12 , 1.1 % C-13 , and 0.0000000001 % C-14 . When it died it , presumably , stopped eating , and now the amounts of C-12 and C-13 are fixed in its body , but not the amount of C-14 , because C-14 decays with a half - life of 5,730±40 years . So , if we measure the relative concentrations of C-12 , C13 , and C-14 in a fossil today and see how much the C-14 concentration differs from the expected concentration of 0.0000000001 % , we can tell how long it has been ( within some margin of error ) since the animal died . Let 's say the government ( who collects a lot of taxes , and has a lot of money to spend ) decides to give you , Joe Taxpayer , $ 50 a month to spend on food . ( well , you and let 's say , 99 other taxpayers in your town ) You and the other 99 go to the grocery store , buy more food , which helps the grocery store pay it 's bills , including wages for its employees and buying groceries from suppliers . Some of that $ 5000 goes to the employees , some go to the farmers and bakers and suppliers , and some goes to the grocery store owner . The employees have a little more money so they can buy more things for themselves . The farmers and bakers and whatnot have more money , so they grow more crops and bake more bread to sell to those employees who have more money . This is a really simple version of " demand - side " economics , and the idea was originally expressed by a guy named Keynes . More money to people means more spending , means more demand , and the economy grows . Trickle - down economics looks at the other side of the equation . It suggests we should give $ 5000 to * the grocery store owner * . With that extra money , the owner can afford to expand the store . Maybe he decides the store can be open for a few more hours a day , and needs to hire more people to cover those hours . Maybe he decides to buy more groceries to get a discount , and lowers his prices . Maybe he just decides to give his employees raises , so they 're more motivated and productive on the job . Basically , he can use the money to grow his business , or make his business more efficient so his employees or his customers ( or both ) get a benefit . The theory behind trickle - down economics comes in large part from something that happened in the 1970 's . For a long time , our government used demand - side economics , like in the first example . Demand - side economics has a known drawback : by increasing demand and having more money in circulation , you create inflation . You can fight inflation by having less money in circulation , but that leads to higher unemployment rates . For a very long time , this was believed to be an absolute truth of economics : if inflation was high , unemployment was low , and vice - versa . But when the oil crisis and the price shocks happened in the 70 's , something unexpected happened : inflation went way up , * * and * * the unemployment rate went up . This happened because the * supply - side * of the economic decreased , something that really had n't happened in a very long time before or since . But it got some people thinking : if its possible to shrink the * supply * and create high inflation * * and * * high unemployment , then should n't it be possible to grow the supply - side , getting both low inflation and low unemployment ? The answer to that turns out to be " it might be possible in theory , but in the real world , no we ca n't reliably grow the Supply part of the economy the way we can consistently stimulate the Demand part of the economy " .

Heisenberg's uncertainty principle is a fundamental idea in quantum mechanics, which is a theory that describes how very small things (like atoms and subatomic particles) behave. According to the uncertainty principle, it is impossible to know both the exact position and the exact momentum (speed and direction) of a particle at the same time. 
This might seem strange, but imagine trying to measure the position of a small insect flying around a room. If you want to know exactly where the insect is, you might try to shine a flashlight on it. But as soon as you shine the light on the insect, you change its momentum (because the light has energy, and when it hits the insect it gives some of that energy to the insect). So now you know where the insect is, but you don't know its momentum as well as you did before. On the other hand, if you don't shine any light on the insect, you might be able to figure out its momentum (for example, by clapping your hands and trying to chase it), but now you don't know its position as well as you did before. 
The uncertainty principle tells us that there is always a trade-off between knowing the position and knowing the momentum of a particle. The more precisely we know one of these things, the less precisely we can know the other. This is a fundamental aspect of the nature of the universe at very small scales, and it has important implications for how we understand the world around us.

Everything alive produces * some * heat just by being alive , even cold blooded things like snakes . The difference between you and a snake is how much heat you produce and how much you have to eat to produce it . You have to eat your three meals a day , probably with a snack or two in between , to keep your energy up . There are snakes that can eat just once every few days because they do n't " waste " any energy on keeping themselves warm . The trade off is they can only live where it 's nice and warm outside all the time , while you can live in a much chillier climate , assuming you keep eating . If you want to know where the actual heat comes from , it has to do with breaking the chemical bonds in the food you eat . Whenever you break a bond between two molecules , a little bit of heat is released . If you want to bond two molecules together , you have to put some heat into the reaction . This breakdown happens in your stomach , and again in every cell in your body .

The reason that propellers are mounted on the back of boats and the front of airplanes is because of the way that these vehicles move through water and air.Boats move through water, which is a dense, viscous fluid. When a boat moves through water, the water pushes against the hull of the boat, providing a forward force that propels the boat forward. To move the boat, the propeller is mounted on the back of the boat and turns, pushing the water behind the boat and propelling the boat forward.Airplanes, on the other hand, fly through the air, which is a much less dense fluid than water. When an airplane moves through the air, it generates lift, which is a force that acts perpendicular to the direction of motion and lifts the airplane off the ground. The lift is generated by the wings of the airplane, which are specially shaped to produce lift when the airplane moves through the air. To move the airplane forward, the airplane has an engine that powers the propeller, which is mounted on the front of the airplane. The propeller turns, pulling the airplane through the air and propelling it forward.So, in short, the reason that propellers are mounted on the back of boats and the front of airplanes is because of the way that these vehicles move through their respective environments. Boats use the propeller to push against the water to move forward, while airplanes use the propeller to pull the airplane through the air to move forward.

The reason is : because that 's how Ptolemy did it . Claudius Ptolemy was a very smart man who lived nearly 1900 years ago . He 's most famous for books about astronomy , but he also wrote a very important book about map - making , which also contained an atlas of the world as it was known to his people , the ancient Greeks . This book is called the * Geographia * . The actual maps that went along with the * Geographia * have n't survived , but that does n't matter because the book lists the coordinates for every place he mentions . Anyway , Ptolemy spends book 1 of the * Geographia * talking about earlier map - makers , and about the mathematical problems that come up when you try to draw the curved surface of the earth on a flat piece of paper . Eventually , at the start of book 2 , he actually starts with the maps . He begins by deciding how he 's going to arrange the map . Now , 1 . his people had more information about the area around the Mediterranean Sea than about the rest of the world , 2 . the Mediterranean is in the north - west of the Eurasian - African landmass , and 3 . in ancient Greek you write from left to right and top to bottom of the page . For these reasons , he decided to put the north - west at the top left . Here 's what he writes : > We propose an arrangement with consideration for what will generally be useful for a drawn map : and that is the system whereby we make movements from left to right , starting from things set down and moving to those not yet taken in hand . This may be done if things to the north are written before those in the south , and things in the west before those in the east ; that is , to the sight of those writing or viewing , north lies up , and the east of the known world lies to the right , both on the globe and on a map . After Ptolemy , western Europeans did n't make decent maps for a long time . But when they got really interested in maps again in the ~~15th~~ 14th century , they used Ptolemy 's * Geographia * as a starting point because it was * that damn good * . So almost all of them followed his practice of putting north - west at the top left . Pretty quickly this became the standard way to do it , and the rest of the world has adopted the same system .

Hiccups are caused by involuntary contractions of the diaphragm, which is the muscle that separates your chest from your abdomen and helps you breathe. They can be triggered by a variety of things, including eating too quickly, swallowing air, drinking carbonated beverages, and being overly full after a meal.

There are a few things you can try to prevent hiccups:

Eat slowly: Take your time when you eat, and try to chew your food thoroughly before swallowing.

Avoid swallowing air: Don't talk while you eat, and try not to gulp down your drinks.

Stay hydrated: Drink plenty of water throughout the day to keep your body hydrated.

Avoid spicy or acidic foods: These types of foods can irritate the lining of your esophagus, which can lead to hiccups.

Don't eat too much: Eat smaller, more frequent meals throughout the day instead of large meals. This can help prevent your stomach from getting overly full, which can cause hiccups.

If you do get hiccups, there are a few things you can try to get rid of them:

Hold your breath: Take a deep breath in, then hold it for as long as you can. This can help relax the diaphragm and stop the hiccups.

Swallow sugar: Swallow a teaspoon of sugar or a small piece of bread. This can help stimulate the nerves in the back of your throat, which can help stop the hiccups.

Drink water: Take small sips of water, or try drinking from the opposite side of a glass. This can help stimulate the nerves in your throat and stop the hiccups.

Gargle with ice water: Gargling with ice water can help stimulate the nerves in your throat and stop the hiccups.

It's important to note that hiccups are usually not a serious problem and will usually go away on their own. If your hiccups persist for more than a few days, or if they are accompanied by other symptoms such as difficulty swallowing or chest pain, it's a good idea to talk to your doctor.

Irony is a way of using language that involves saying something that is opposite to what you really mean, or that is the opposite of what you expect to happen. There are three main types of irony:

Verbal irony: This is when you say something but mean the opposite. For example, if someone says "Great, just what I needed, another meeting," when they are actually annoyed about having to attend a meeting, they are using verbal irony.

Situational irony: This is when something happens that is the opposite of what you would expect. For example, if a fire station burns down, that is situational irony because you would expect a fire station to prevent fires, not be the victim of one.

Dramatic irony: This is when the audience knows something that the characters in a story do not. For example, if a character in a play is unaware that a bomb is about to go off, but the audience knows, that is dramatic irony.

Irony can be used for a variety of purposes, such as to make a joke, to be sarcastic, or to create a sense of mystery or suspense. It is important to use irony correctly, as misusing it can lead to confusion or misunderstanding.

The unit of pressure called "millimeters of mercury" (mmHg) is still used today because it is a very convenient unit to use for certain types of pressure measurements. Now, imagine that you put a hole in the bottom of the tube and let some of the liquid out. As the liquid comes out of the hole, it creates a pressure, or force, on the inside of the tube. The more liquid that comes out of the hole, the more pressure there is inside the tube. So, when we measure pressure in millimeters of mercury, we are basically measuring how much liquid is being forced out of a hole in a tube. This is a very useful way to measure pressure because it is easy to do and doesn't require any special equipment. It is also a very accurate way to measure pressure because the density of mercury is very constant, which means that the weight of a certain volume of mercury will always be the same. It may seem like a strange or "jerry-rigged" unit, but it has actually been very useful for many years and is still used today in many different fields.

Sure! DES and AES are types of encryption that are used to protect information. Encryption is like a secret code that can be used to send messages or store data in a way that only people with the right "key" can understand it. Imagine you have a secret message that you want to send to your friend, but you don't want anyone else to be able to read it. You could write the message down on a piece of paper and then put it in a locked box. To make the message even more secure, you could also scramble the letters of the message so that it looks like a bunch of random letters. This way, even if someone else finds the box and opens it, they won't be able to read the message because it's all scrambled. DES and AES are like the locked box and the scrambled letters. They use complex math to turn your message into a bunch of random-looking numbers and letters, and then only someone with the right "key" can turn the message back into something that makes sense. So, to summarize: DES and AES are ways to protect information by turning it into a secret code that only people with the right key can understand.

An amp (ampere) is a unit of electrical current. It measures the flow of electrons in a circuit. 
A volt (voltage) is a unit of electrical potential difference. It measures the pressure that drives electrons through a circuit. 
A watt is a unit of power. It measures the rate at which energy is transferred or used. 
Here's an example to help you understand the difference between these three units: 
Imagine a water pipe with water flowing through it. The amps would be like the amount of water flowing through the pipe. The volts would be like the pressure of the water. And the watts would be like the rate at which the water is flowing and doing work (such as turning a turbine to generate electricity). 
So, in short: 
Amps measure the flow of electrons (current).
Volts measure the pressure that drives the electrons through the circuit (voltage).
Watts measure the rate at which energy is being transferred or used (power).

There are two big hurdles : making sure that the brain is still viable after transplant and that doctors can actually re - attach the brain . What modern medicine does know is that approximately 15 - 20 seconds after your heart stops , you lose consciousness . In 10 minutes ( 20 minutes if certain drugs are used ) , your neurons are * damaged * , not dead , from oxygen deprivation . Doctors do not know exactly how long it takes for neurons to die from lack of oxygen , only that extended time without oxygen will cause brain damage and the accompanying physiological results . What * will * kill your brain cells , however , is [ reperfusion injury ] ( URL_0 ) . After cells are deprived of oxygen , the sudden reintroduction of oxygen causes oxidation reactions and release of free radicals ( see the link for a paper on it . ) Now , assuming that the brain is fully intact and functional after being transplanted ( avoiding reperfusion injury and the like ) , the issue is connecting it to the spinal cord . Again , I 'm assuming that there is no damage due to trauma , infection , oxygen deprivation , etc . Currently , there are no successful means of re - establishing the connection to the spinal cord . Even with oxygen reaching the brain and life support for the rest of your new body 's systems , you 'd basically be a ghost in a shell .

If you want kids to eat veggies , you have to learn to make them appealing in their presentation . Do n't just dump some mushy peas on a plate and say " eat it , this is good for you " , make their food bright and fun and invite them to make it with you . Kids also love being involved and feeling like they can choose their food . Sit them on the bench when preparing a salad and ask them if they want to try some carrot or cucumber for example . if you kid refuses to try veggies , simply hide them in foods they will eat ( ie if they hate eggplant then fry some in olive oil and put it on a pizza or stir some through pasta ) . It 's important not to get mad at them for not eating fruit or veggies , be encouraging and positive . If you have a negative reaction like forcing the food in their mouths and forcing them to swallow it , that will put them off further as they 'll associate vegetables with fear and anxiety . Kids do n't really have an ingrained aversion to veggies , it 's just that they are very easily influenced by tv , their peers and pop culture . They see kids on tv turning their nose up at veggies and feeding their face with sweets , they 'll do the same . They are bombarded with ads for sugary , greasy foods , they see kids their age on tv eating at McDonalds , etc . It 's everywhere so you ca n't really stop them seeing it unless you have them in isolation . What you can do is lead by example . Make sure they see you enjoying fresh fruit and veg and drinking water rather than consuming fast food , sweets and soda . Some tips to make their food appealing : * has to be fresh and colourful . * if you want your kid to eat cabbage , shred it and mix it in a colourful salad . * Sit your kid on the bench when cooking and encourage them to try out the ingredients . * Grow your own fruits and vegetables . Kids love getting mucky , encourage them to plant the seeds and water and weed the garden . * They might turn their nose up the first time because it 's unfamiliar . Keep offering it . If they still refuse it , try serving it another way ( ie they do n't like fresh kiwi but they might like it in a smoothie ) * Offer fruit like strawberries and grapes instead of sweets . * Blindfold them and put a piece of fruit or vegetable in their mouth , get them to identify the flavour and ask them what they think they 're eating . Sometimes their refusal to eat something might be based on appearance , this is one way to get past that . * give them foods in their favourite colour ( if they like red then offer them apples , red peppers , strawberries , cherries , etc ) * Make up stories about kids who like a food they hate ( ie " one upon a time , there was a little girl called Henrietta who loved spinach " )

A 512 MB SD card has a smaller capacity and can hold fewer files, while a 64 GB SD card has a much larger capacity and can hold many more files. Memory capacities tend to get larger and cheaper over time because of something called "Moore's Law." Moore's Law is a prediction made by Gordon Moore, one of the founders of Intel, in 1965. He noticed that the number of transistors (tiny switches that are used to store and process information) on a computer chip was doubling every few years. This meant that computers were getting faster and more powerful, and memory capacities were also increasing. As technology has advanced, the number of transistors that can be packed onto a single chip has continued to increase, which has allowed for the production of larger and cheaper memory cards.

The bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945, which led to the end of World War II, was not considered a war crime at the time because it was not prohibited by international law. At the time, the use of atomic weapons was not specifically prohibited, and the United States argued that the bombings were necessary to bring a swift end to the war and save lives that would have been lost in a longer conflict or invasion of Japan. 
However, many people today view the use of atomic weapons as a moral outrage, and some have argued that they should be considered a war crime in retrospect. Much of the research on sleep involves EEGs , or Electroencephalography , which involves recording electrical activity on one 's head . Other electrodes can also be used to monitor other aspects of the body , such as heart rate , respiration , or skin conductance during sleep . We can physiologically characterize sleep based on different waveforms going on in the brain . When we are * * Awake * * , EEGs show two different patterns of activity , which we can call A activity and B activity ( short for alpha and beta ) . A activity shows up as medium - frequency waves , and occurs when we are at rest ( but awake ) , sort of just hanging out not really doing anything . B activity consists of irregular , low amplitude waves , and shows up when we are alert and attentive to things in our environment , or thinking actively . Now when we go to sleep , we start to get drowsy and enter * * Stage 1 sleep * * , characterized by T activity ( theta ) , which is really a transition from sleep to wakefulness . Your eyes are rolling , and eyelids may slowly open and close . 10 minutes later you 'll enter * * Stage 2 sleep * * , where the EEG is irregular but has some T activity , but also is characterized by sleep spindles and K complexes , which are two other waveforms we can distinguish in Stage 2 ( especially K complexes , which are also the forerunners of Delta waves , which show up in deep sleep ) . K complexes are like sharp wave spikes ( like a big ocean wave ) in an otherwise lower ocean of waveforms . So you 're definitely asleep now ( in the example , hopefully not from reading!) ... if you were awoken now , you probably would deny that you have actually been asleep for around 15 - 30 minutes ! You are entering * * Stage 3 sleep * * now , characterized by the occurrence of D activity ( delta ) , where the theta waves are really startin ' a rockin ' , large amplitudes , big crashing theta waves . * * Stage 4 sleep * * , or slow wave sleep , contains only D activity . This goes on for a few more minutes , and then , about 90 minutes after the beginning of sleep , we notice a crazy change : the EEG becomes very desynchronized , which only a few theta waves , and it actually starts to look like Stage 1 , or even like being awake ! The EEG ( or other measures , like the EOG or EMG , for eye movement or muscle activity ) become very silent . The person becomes somewhat paralyzed here , and the cornea in the eye produces a big bulge that can be seen to move about ( by the EOG ) . This time is what is referred to as * * REM sleep * * . The odd thing about REM ( rapid eye movement ; stages 1 - 4 are called non - rem sleep , btw ) sleep is that while this is the time where people report having the most vivid , narrative , story - like dreams , the EEG looks like we 're awake ! If you were woken up in Stage 3 - 4 , you might not report having been dreaming , but this is the deepest sleep stage , and it will be hard to wake you up ( and you will be very groggy ! ) During the rest of the night you will alternate between REM and non - REM sleep , with each cycle being AROUND 90 minutes long , with 20 - 30 minute bouts of REM sleep . So an 8 - hour sleep cycle will have 4 - 5 periods of REM sleep , and periods of REM sleep INCREASE as the night goes on , while periods of slow - wave sleep ( stage 4 especially ) , tend to only occur in the early hours of the night . Aircraft can fly at low altitude which would mean they did n't need to be pressurised but it would be a ) very loud and b ) much less fuel efficient because the atmosphere is thicker than at high altitude . You already got your answer , so I 'll throw in an extra tidbit : most planets are n't perfectly round . They 're slightly chubbier around the equator and slightly flattened near the poles , like when you squeeze a rubber ball from the top and botom . This us due to the fact that they rotate around their own axis . The rotation creates a centrifugal force that increases the radius of the planet along the plane of rotation , and gravity causes the matter along the axis to " settle down " into this new shape . This is also the reason why most galaxies have a flattened - out spiral shape , instead of being big globular clusters of stars .

I often spend time thinking in direct concepts , without language . It 's the only way to think with great speed , and it 's really essential for attaining new insights in any topic . Just a quick illustration : suddenly realize that you need to go shopping before the weekend . Then , instantly after realizing this , you automatically say to yourself , silently in your head " I need to go shopping before this weekend . " OK , now do the same , but DON'T say anything to yourself . Just notice the pure concept of intending to go to the store , and the badness connected with failing to do so before the weekend . ( Best of all , these realizations happen instantly , and you do n't need to take the time to construct any long english sentence in your head . ) Or another : write a short essay about some topic . Then write another one on exactly the same topic , but in a totally different way ; using a different approach . Then do it a third time . The plans / concepts / connections / intent never changed , yet the words came out totally different . Words are n't thinking , words were chosen later . There was a time though when all my own thinking was always " self - talk . " But I realized that it was very slow thinking , and also was totally destroying my creativity , so I gradually went back to the way I 'd been as a kid . Back before age 9 or so , I thought in concepts , and only used words when I was talking to others . So , to answer your question yourself , just try to remember what it was like being six years old . That , or try to remember your dreams in detail . ( For me at least , during dreams I 'm not thinking in words . As I wake up , all the " self talk " tends to turn back on . )

A 4D hypercube is also known as a tesseract. It is a higher-dimensional version of a cube, with four dimensions instead of three.

Imagine a cube in three-dimensional space. If you were to extend this cube into a fourth dimension, it would create a new set of edges and faces that are perpendicular to the original edges and faces of the cube. These new edges and faces would form a new, smaller cube within the original cube. This smaller cube is the 3D projection of the 4D tesseract into our 3D space.

So, when we represent a 4D tesseract, we show it as a cube within a cube to give a sense of the extra dimensions that are present. It can be a little difficult to visualize a 4D object, so the cube-within-a-cube representation helps to give a better understanding of what the tesseract looks like.

The Holocaust was a horrific event in which millions of people, including Jews, Romani, homosexuals, disabled people, and others considered undesirable by the Nazi regime, were systematically murdered during World War II. Despite this overwhelming evidence, some people choose to deny that the Holocaust ever happened. There are a few reasons why drug testing is not currently required for people to receive welfare benefits (also known as "public assistance"). One reason is that it is expensive to implement drug testing programs. The government would have to pay for the tests, as well as the staff to administer them and process the results. This would be a lot of money, especially if a large number of people were being tested. Another reason is that drug testing might not be very effective at achieving its goals. For example, some people who use drugs might be able to pass a drug test, while others who don't use drugs might test positive due to false positives or other issues with the testing process. This could lead to people being denied benefits unfairly. Finally, some people think that drug testing for welfare is not a good idea because it might discourage people from seeking help when they need it. If people are worried that they might be drug tested and lose their benefits if they test positive, they might be less likely to apply for assistance in the first place. This could make it harder for people who really need help to get it. So, overall, the decision not to drug test for welfare is based on a combination of cost, effectiveness, and fairness concerns.

Artificial intelligence (AI) can learn in a number of ways, but one common method is through machine learning. In machine learning, a computer is given a set of data and a task to perform. The computer is not explicitly programmed to perform the task, but instead uses the data to learn how to do it.

For example, a machine learning model might be given a large collection of images and told to identify which ones contain dogs. The model would analyze the data and look for patterns or features that are common in images of dogs. It would then use these patterns to make predictions about new images it has never seen before.

The programmer does not control the specific patterns the model looks for or how it makes predictions. Instead, they provide the data and the task, and the model "learns" on its own how to perform the task. However, the programmer does have to choose the right type of model and set it up correctly in order to get good results.

Another way that AI can learn is through reinforcement learning, where a computer is given a goal and rewards or punishments for reaching or not reaching that goal. The computer learns through trial and error, adjusting its actions based on the rewards or punishments it receives.

In both of these cases, the AI is learning by itself, rather than being directly controlled by the programmer. However, the programmer still plays an important role in setting up the learning process and making sure it is working correctly.

We know the height and location of Everest 's peak the same way we know most of the terrain around us , through surveying . The simplest explanation of surveying is that you start with two reference points that you know the location of * very * accurately . It does n't really matter which two points , but you especially want to know the distance between them super accurately . From there , you can locate any third point that is visible from the first two by measuring the angles between them . Any triangle adds up to 180 degrees , after all . You can figure out distances because you know the angles , and you know one distance very accurately , and sine and cosine are your friends . And you can measure relative heights by having a vertical reference , like weight that hangs on a cord , and measuring the angle from it as well . Once you have a third point , you can use any pair of points to generate a fourth . And any two of those four to generate a fifth , and so on . Eventually you have a bunch of triangles radiating out from your starting two points , and you can measure the whole country or whole world this way . In reality , there are lots of known points used in surveying , that we 've located in a variety of ways . But we still locate new points off these known points in the same way , with triangulation by line of sight .

Ebola is a virus that causes a severe and often deadly disease in humans and nonhuman primates. It is thought to be transmitted to people from animals, such as bats or monkeys, and spreads from person to person through direct contact with the blood or bodily fluids of an infected person. The survival rate for Ebola varies depending on several factors, including the strain of the virus, the person's age and overall health, and the availability of medical treatment. It is not necessarily the case that only 10% of people survive Ebola. In fact, the survival rate for Ebola can be as high as 90% or more in some outbreaks, while in other outbreaks it may be lower. There is no known genetic defense that only a small percentage of people have that enables them to survive Ebola. However, it is possible that some people may have a stronger immune system or other factors that help them better fight off the virus. It is also possible that people who receive prompt and appropriate medical care, such as fluids to prevent dehydration and medications to help manage symptoms, may have a better chance of survival.

Blood types are a way to categorize people based on the types of proteins called "antigens" found on the surface of their red blood cells. Humans have four blood types: A, B, AB, and O. We have blood types because they help keep our bodies healthy. For example, if you get a blood transfusion (when you receive blood from someone else), it's important that the blood you receive is compatible with your own blood type. If the blood is not compatible, your body could have a reaction and you might get sick.

Other animals can also have different blood types, but the specific blood types and antigens they have can be different from those found in humans. For example, some animals have blood types based on different proteins or sugars. Some animals, such as dogs, also have subtypes of blood within their blood types.

Diplomatic immunity is a legal principle that gives certain foreign government officials immunity from prosecution in the host country where they are serving. This immunity applies to both civil and criminal cases. It is intended to allow diplomats to do their job without fear of legal repercussions in the host country. 
However, diplomatic immunity is not a "get out of jail free" card. It does not give diplomats the right to break the law or commit crimes with impunity. If a diplomat commits a serious crime, such as murder, the host country has the right to waive the diplomat's immunity and allow them to be prosecuted. 
In general, the country where the diplomat is from is responsible for disciplining or punishing the diplomat if they have committed a crime. The host country can request that the diplomat be recalled to their home country to face charges, but the home country is not required to do so. 
In summary, diplomatic immunity is a legal principle that gives certain foreign government officials immunity from prosecution in the host country where they are serving, but it does not cover all crimes and can be waived if a diplomat commits a serious crime.

Deja vu is the feeling that you have experienced something before, even if you know that you have not. It can be a strange and unsettling feeling, and it is not fully understood by scientists.

One theory is that deja vu happens when there is a glitch in the brain's memory system. Our brains are constantly processing and storing information about the world around us, and sometimes this information can get mixed up or stored in the wrong place. This can lead to the feeling that we have experienced something before, even though we actually have not.

Affected and effected are two words that are often confused because they sound similar and can be used in similar contexts. However, they have different meanings and are used in different ways.

Affected is an adjective that means influenced or changed by something. For example, you might say "The storm affected the power lines, causing a blackout." In this sentence, the storm influenced or changed the power lines.

Effected is a verb that means to cause something to happen or to bring about a change. For example, you might say "The new policy was effected last month." In this sentence, the new policy caused a change to happen.

In the United States, sweepstakes and contests are regulated by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), which is a government agency that protects consumers from deceptive or misleading practices. According to the FTC, a sweepstakes is a game of chance in which a prize is awarded to a winner or winners selected by chance. This means that if a company offers a sweepstakes or contest that requires a purchase to enter, it must also offer a way for consumers to enter without making a purchase, commonly known as a "no purchase necessary" option. 
For example, if a restaurant like Subway or McDonald's offers a sticker peel game in which customers can win a prize by collecting a certain number of stickers with their purchases, the restaurant must also provide a way for customers to enter the game without making a purchase. This could include, for example, mailing in a request to enter or visiting the restaurant's website and filling out an online entry form. 
In summary, "no purchase necessary" means that a consumer can enter a sweepstakes or contest without making a purchase, as long as they follow the instructions provided by the company for entering without making a purchase.

Zeno's Paradox of the Tortoise is a thought experiment that is meant to challenge our common sense understanding of motion and time. In the paradox, Zeno imagines a race between a tortoise and the hero Achilles. The tortoise has a head start, and Achilles gives chase. However, Zeno argues that Achilles can never catch up to the tortoise, because before he can reach the tortoise's starting point, the tortoise will have moved a little bit forward. And before Achilles can reach that point, the tortoise will have moved forward again, and so on.

This might seem confusing, but the idea is that no matter how fast Achilles runs, there will always be a distance between him and the tortoise. So, according to Zeno's argument, it is impossible for Achilles to ever catch up to the tortoise.

Now, this might seem to be in conflict with Newtonian Kinematics, which is a branch of physics that studies how objects move. According to Newton's laws of motion, an object will keep moving at a constant speed in a straight line unless a force acts on it to change its motion. So, in the race between the tortoise and Achilles, we would expect Achilles to be able to catch up to the tortoise as long as he runs fast enough.

However, Zeno's Paradox of the Tortoise is not really meant to be taken literally. It is a philosophical argument that is meant to challenge our understanding of time and motion. So, even though it might seem to be in conflict with Newtonian Kinematics, it is really just a thought experiment that is meant to make us think about these concepts in a different way.

This post pertains primarily to the rise of Reddit more so than the others . Back when I was a teenager , sites like URL_1 , CollegeHumor , and eBaum 's World were the places to go for funny stuff . Usually comments on posts and things there would link to other sites , so a word - of - mouth type thing . One of the popular sites in those days was Digg . Digg was a lot like reddit , with user submitted content and up and down votes to determine exposure ( IIRC anyway , obviously been a while ) . Digg was a fantastically popular website in its heyday , and generated tons of traffic . However , they went the way of Icarus and flew too close to the sun . They were n't content with their advertising revenue , and started curating sponsored posts in exchange for money . This led to Digg deleting posts , changing content , and hiding submissions , since the companies paying did n't want their brand associated with anything controversial . It turned very much into a " walled garden , " and word about the new fangled Reddit quickly spread through comment sections and internet forums . Unfortunately , this seems to be a natural life cycle . The last few years have seen Reddit adopt a similar business model ( just google " Online Reputation Management " and see how many companies are in this business now ) . A few examples from just the last 12 months would be the Rick & Morty viral marketing campaign , the McDonald 's Szechuan sauce campaign , tons of The_Donald , and lots of the Resist / Left Wing / Bernie stuff everywhere . The entire Tiny Trumps sub is the best example . Go back and look at some of the original posts on the sub , the user accounts are usually several years old , but only began posting in 2016 , and all political . [ This Forbes article ] ( URL_0 ) goes into greater detail about it . Political astroturfing is nothing new , and both sides are guilty of it . Unfortunately their tug of war over Reddit has a great chance of eventually collapsing the platform . The long and short of it is basically websites featuring user driven content are cool . People like and use them . Companies that specialize in " online reputation management " create thousands of accounts and leave them dormant for months . When the site reaches a certain level of popularity , these reputation management companies are hired for viral advertising . This becomes standard practice until the bigger content creators no longer use the platform . Disaffected users eventually find each other through forums and comment sections , and the next big website is born . It develops through its infancy , and is eventually sucked back into the web of viral astroturfing . The platform is destroyed , users flee , and the Phoenix is reborn .

Dry cleaning is a method of cleaning clothes and fabrics that uses special chemicals instead of water to remove dirt and stains. The process is called "dry" cleaning because it uses no water.

Here's how it works: First, the clothes are carefully examined to identify any stains or areas that need special attention. Then, the clothes are placed in a machine called a dry cleaning machine. This machine looks like a big drum that spins around.

Inside the dry cleaning machine, the clothes are cleaned using a special chemical solvent instead of water. The solvent is very effective at removing dirt and stains from clothes, but it's also very gentle, so it won't damage the fabric.

After the clothes have been cleaned with the solvent, they are rinsed with a small amount of water to remove any remaining dirt or chemicals. Then, they are dried using hot air and pressed to make them look nice and fresh.

So, while the process is called "dry" cleaning, it does involve a small amount of water at the end. But overall, it's a very gentle and effective way to clean clothes without using a lot of water.

This is a perpetual motion machine . They are impossible as they violate the second law of thermodynamics , in that you can not get out more energy than is put in . No machine is perfect , there will always be losses in the form of heat and motion . If a motor were to drive a generator , the losses come in the form of heat due to electrical resistance and bearing friction , and eddy currents opposing rotation of both the motor and the generator . So all a car can do is try to minimize loss by reducing aerodynamic and rolling resistance , recapture inefficiency through regenerative breaking and coasting , and utilize free energy in the form of solar ( though right now no production vehicle has solar panels on the sun bearing surfaces because they 're not yet that cost effective , you only see this in solar racers , and those " cars " are made of balsa wood and weigh 300 kg or less ) .

Another variation of this , to add to the Like I 'm Five - ness : Imagine that you really like the song " Twinkle Twinkle Little Star " . The original is made on Piano . We know the tempo , the notes and the sequence they should be played in . Should be pretty easy to bring it over to another instrument , right ? Well , sort of . Now we want to play it on Saxaphone . But there 's a lot of things different here . You ca n't just push keys , there 's also a method of blowing into this instrument . In the grand scheme of things , the tempo and notes are the same , but what 's involved to reach those same chords is significantly different . But now that we 're proficient at two instruments , it should be super easy to bring it over to violin , right ? Well , no . Violin requires a significant amount of practice and control . Again , the way we generate the sound and how to control it has changed significantly . Even if the song remains the same , the way we produce the song is very different . That is , and has been , the core problem with developing on PC , Xbox 360 and PS3 . All three platforms use different processor architectures , which means you ca n't just ' take code ' from one thing and put it on another . This is n't like taking coke and pouring it into a pepsi bottle . This is like trying to put a Saxaphone into a violin case . Good luck with that . So even though things like art and sound can be carried over as is in many cases , the actual engine of the game and how things like lighting , texture rendering and physics work .. those often have to be built from the ground up . Major publishers have an advantage of licensing third - party engines such as Unreal or creating their own engine across multiple platforms , making it much easier to produce a game across multiple platforms . But smaller studios that do n't secure multi - platform deals , often do n't have the extra time , manpower and money to create and do quality assurance across multiple platforms . Now , referring to your initial question about XBOne demos being done on high end PCs ... That was , allegedly , only one game demo for " Loco Cycle " . The developers of that title outright confessed that they had not finished porting their code to the XBOne by E3 and were showing their game on high end PCs . This largely debunks the claims that ' many ' games at E3 were running on high end PCs ( even though Battlefield 4 clearly had some keyboard prompts on screen ) . But here 's the good news : Whereas PC , Xbox 360 ( PowerPC architecture ) and PS4 ( Cell architecture ) were all running separate processor types , the ' Next Gen ' of consoles will all be running the same processor type . x86_64 ! If you 've purchased a PC in the last 3 years , chances are it 's one of these . A multi - core , 64 bit capable processor . Reports from indie developers so far are great . It only took the Octodad team 4 months to get their game playable on the PS4 after porting it from the PC , and most of the porting involved adjusting shaders ( for the proprietary graphics chip ) . This * could * mean many more multi - platform ports of games . Not just more of them , but also higher quality and faster cross - platform releases as well . Will it shake out that way ? Maybe . It 's hard to say . There 's still an unfortunate myth about the high cost of PC gaming ( protip : you could build a PC that 'll play Crysis 3 with better than Xbox 360 graphics for about the price of an XboxOne , and it 'll do all that other PC stuff ) . Because of this , there is a significant gap between the number of people that have " gaming PCs " and the number of people that own consoles . To be fair , PCs take more maintenance and tech savvy to run smoothly compared to consoles , and that 's something many people prefer not to deal with . Even though PC ownership vastly outnumbers console ownership , PCs equipped to pump out DX11 graphics are a meager percentage of that install base . Even though the PC gaming market may potentially be smaller than the console market , it 's still a viable market . Despite claims of rampant piracy ' ruining the platform ' , it 's clear publishers still make decent dollars from it as just about every game that was n't platform exclusive in recent months also came out on PC . Call of Duty , Battlefield , Crysis , FarCry , Hitman , Tomb Raider , etc . If a singular processor architecture makes porting titles easier for game designers , it 's a sure bet that publishers will seek to push their wares onto every possible platform they can profit from . Licensing exclusives aside ( That includes The Last of Us ) , I anticipate seeing a whole lot more games becoming multi - platform in the future , and the distinction between consoles and PC being licensed exclusives , rather than technology barriers .

Ah , something I can explain ! Timeshare means you are purchasing a " share " of a property . Sometimes this is a simple agreement among friends / business partners . For example ... * I want to buy a house at the beach . I 've got $ 150 K saved up , but I ca n't qualify for a loan on a second home . I have four people in similar situations , so we decide to pool our resources . We each put in $ 150 K for a total of $ 750K. That buys us a nice place , ocean front , at the beach . Costs are shared - insurance , taxes , etc . We agree to some sort of selection system , where maybe I want the house for two weeks in June and a month at Christmas ... but Bob wants the house for three weeks in August ( and so on , account for all weeks , everyone agrees to an equitable split ) . And maybe we rent it out a little at peak weeks , just to recoup some of the investment . If we die , our " interest " in that house is transferred to a spouse or child ... or can we can sell the 1/5 share . We just make sure a lawyer creates a robust contract to protect us all . Now , that used to be more common ... but it 's a fairly rare thing to see now . But I want to use it as a simplistic example so when we talk about what timeshares * usually * look like , you 've got a simpler reference . Here 's how modern timeshares typically work : * You buy " points " with a timeshare company that owns many properties . Let 's say for example that 100,000 points costs $ 10 K ( for simplicity ) , and your points are set to the establishment where you bought them ( i.e. the company owns multiple properties , but you have a " home " property for those points ) . Let 's say you bought the points at the Aspen , CO time share office , because you like to go skiing every February for a week . That costs 96,000 points to book , so ( buying in blocks ) you pay $ 10 K to get 100,000 points ( this means you get 100,000 points EACH YEAR - automatically ) , and that 's enough points to get your week in Aspen every year . You pay upkeep charges on the unit too , might be $ 1,000 per year . Ok , so you 're set , one week in Aspen for the rest of your life ( not a set week , you still need to schedule it far in advance ) ... you bought in at $ 10 K ( which you can sell back at some point ) plus a $ 1 K annual fee to maintain the unit . When you visit Aspen , you need to pre - arrange the week you 're going well in advance , and you do not have a set unit . * Let 's say you do n't feel like skiing this year ... you could bank the points for next year , and go somewhere for TWO weeks ! Or skip this year , and use the rollover points for somewhere nicer next year ( a luxury unit in Hawaii for example ) . Or maybe you just want to trade , to take the kids to Disney . Easy , you call companies main office and tell them what you want to do . You 're probably getting preferred status at your " home " office in Aspen , but it 's a big company , lots of properties , assuming you 're planning appropriately in advance and have enough points to do it - you can go anywhere they have properties . Also , units can be " rented " to non - timeshare owners . This lets them fill up vacant units . A few other notes : * One way to " game " the system is to have your " home " property be somewhere with lower maintenance fees . Beachfront units in a hurricane region have WAY higher fees than a ski resort would . And a ski resort will be far higher than even a simple lake property in Montana . You run the risk of not getting preference where you * really * want to go ( Florida ) but you will probably be able to get a unit , and you still only pay your " home " unit 's maintenance fee . * Usually we 're talking about condo style units , NOT houses ( with a big company ) . These just look like nice apartment complexes ( outdoor covered entry usually , maybe a more " hotel - like " interior hall if it 's a busier urban property or a converted hotel ) . When you trade in your car, you are essentially selling it to the dealership or the person you are trading it in to. If you still owe money on the car, that means you have not fully paid off the loan or financing agreement that you used to purchase the car.

If you trade in your car before it is fully paid off, the dealership or person you are trading it in to will usually pay off the remaining balance on the loan or financing agreement for you. They will then subtract the amount they paid off from the value of the car when they calculate how much they will give you for the trade-in.

For example, let's say you owe $5,000 on your car and the dealership thinks it is worth $10,000 when you trade it in. The dealership will pay off the $5,000 loan for you and then give you $5,000 for the trade-in (which is the value of the car minus the amount they paid off on the loan).

So, when you trade in your car while you still owe money on it, you are essentially selling the car and using the money you get from the sale to pay off the remaining balance on the loan. This can be a good option if you want to get a new car but don't have the money to pay off the loan on your current car outright. However, it is important to be aware that you may not get as much money for the trade-in as you would if you waited until the car was fully paid off, since the dealership or person you are trading it in to will need to cover the remaining balance on the loan.

Pickling is a way of preserving vegetables by putting them in a mixture of vinegar, salt, and water. The vinegar and salt help to prevent the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms that can cause food to spoil. The vegetables are placed in a jar or other container, and the pickling mixture is poured over them. The jar is then sealed and left to sit for a period of time, typically a few weeks to a few months. During this time, the vinegar, salt, and water work together to preserve the vegetables and give them a distinctive, tangy flavor. Pickling is a simple and effective way of preserving vegetables, and it has been used for centuries by people all around the world.

The order of operations exists to make sure that mathematical calculations are done in a consistent and correct way.

Imagine you have a math problem that looks like this: 4 + 3 x 2. If you just start from the left and do the operations in order, you would get 10. But if you follow the order of operations, you would first do the multiplication and then the addition, which gives you the correct answer of 7.

The order of operations is a set of rules that tells you the correct order in which to do different math operations. It's like a recipe for making sure your math calculations are done correctly. The order of operations is usually remembered using the acronym PEMDAS:

P: Parentheses first
E: Exponents (ie Powers and Square Roots, etc.)
MD: Multiplication and Division (left-to-right)
AS: Addition and Subtraction (left-to-right)

Following the order of operations helps to prevent mistakes and makes it easier for other people to understand and check your work. It's an important tool in math and it's used in many different areas, from basic arithmetic to advanced calculus.

A null hypothesis is a statement that says that there is no relationship between two things being studied. It is used as a starting point for statistical testing in research. For example, if a researcher is studying whether a new medication is effective at reducing blood pressure, the null hypothesis might be that the medication has no effect on blood pressure. It is important to use a null hypothesis because it allows the researcher to determine whether the results of the study are due to chance or if there is a real relationship between the two things being studied. Sometimes, researchers will test more than one null hypothesis. This is because there might be multiple possible explanations for the results of the study, and the researcher wants to see if any of them are true. Null hypotheses are an important part of the scientific process because they help researchers to be objective and to carefully consider all possible explanations for their results.

Some people argue that death taxes are unfair because they can take a large chunk of money away from the people who are inheriting the assets. Others argue that death taxes are necessary because they help to reduce wealth inequality and ensure that wealthy people contribute to the support of public services.There are different rules and rates for death taxes in different countries and states. Some places do not have death taxes at all. In the United States, for example, the federal government and some states have death taxes, but the rules and rates vary.

If you die and do not have any living relatives, the money in your bank account will not automatically go to the bank or the government. Instead, it will be considered part of your "estate," which is all the property and assets you owned at the time of your death. The process for handling your estate and distributing your assets, including any money in your bank account, is called "probate." 
Probate is a legal process that is carried out by the court. It involves verifying your will (if you have one) and identifying and valuing your assets. The court will then appoint a person called an "executor" to manage the distribution of your assets according to your will or, if you don't have a will, according to the laws of your state. 
If you don't have a will and don't have any living relatives, the court will follow the laws of your state to determine who should receive your assets. In some cases, the state may take possession of your assets and use them for public purposes. In other cases, your assets may be given to certain charities or organizations. 
It's important to have a will and to keep it up to date, so that you can specify who you want to receive your assets after you die. This can help ensure that your money and other assets are distributed according to your wishes.

Here's how it works: each eye has a lens that focuses light onto the retina, which is a layer of special cells at the back of the eye. The retina contains light-sensitive cells called rods and cones, which send signals to the brain when they are stimulated by light. The brain then processes these signals and creates an image of what the animal is seeing. In animals with independent eyes, each eye has its own lens, retina, and set of rods and cones. This means that each eye is able to send separate signals to the brain, which allows the animal to see two different things at the same time. For example, a chameleon might use one eye to look at a predator while using the other eye to keep an eye on its surroundings. Sure! Osmosis is the movement of water from an area where it is more concentrated to an area where it is less concentrated. Imagine you have a cup with sugar dissolved in the water. If you put a straw in the cup, the water in the straw would be less sweet than the water in the cup because there is less sugar in the straw. The water in the straw would want to move to the cup to balance out the concentration of sugar. That movement of water is osmosis. Another way to think about it is that osmosis is like a game of "follow the leader." The water molecules are the players, and they want to be where other water molecules are. If there are more water molecules on one side of a membrane (like the straw), then the water molecules on the other side will try to move over to that side to be with the other water molecules. Short Answer : It does n't actually make you more awake , it just keeps your body from realizing it 's tired . Longer Answer : In your brain , you have these chemicals and chemical receptors . When the chemicals lodge themselves in to the receptor , the receptor activates , and tells your brain that you 're tired . Caffeine looks a lot like this chemical , and lodges itself in to the receptor , blocking the REAL " tired " chemical from doing so . However , since caffeine is the wrong chemical , the receptor it is in never gets activated , and does n't ever tell your brain that you 're tired . When all of these receptors get clogged up with caffeine , your brain has no way of knowing it 's supposed to be tired . tl;dr , Caffeine makes you not - tired ; it DOESN'T make you more awake .
