The voting rights act was necessary at the time but the act itself was not particularly well constructed looking forward . The act lists a large number of municipalities , counties and states which imposed barriers to voting ( EG literacy tests ) but made the process for getting off the list extremely cumbersome such that nearly every area covered no longer has even the hint of racially based voting problems yet is still included . The justice department have also been extraordinarily reluctant to remove areas from the list even when they have clearly demonstrated they no longer have the same issues too ; New Hampshire had to sue the federal government to get its municipalities off the list despite the state having had decades of some of the safest elections in the country . The act has also been supplanted by other legislation which entirely negates the problems it was seeking to solve , the federal government has authority to intercede in state & local elections which is did not 50 years ago such that abusive voting practices would result in a federal court overturning elections . Even if a state managed to pass a literacy test requirement today ( pretty much not a chance ) they would find themselves in a federal court about 5 minutes later , since 1982 all the federal government needs to do to prevent such a practice is simply pass a federal statute barring it . The reason why municipalities / counties / states want off the list is because it attaches stigma that may no longer be accurate ( they must hate black people , the states not permitted to manage its own elections ) and the pre - clearance procedures are both cumbersome and take an extremely long time to traverse . A municipality looking to change the wording on its ballots is looking at a couple of years and hundreds of thousands of dollars in legal fees in order to do so . In many cases this is too expensive to be justifiable so many municipalities are stuck with 50 year old voting procedures which may make elections less safe not more . In terms of why SCOTUS overturned this provision now they felt the renewal violated the 10th amendment as the data being used to justify the act was 50 years old , the 10th and 15th amendments clash in this area so in order for a federal statute to be enforceable over the 10th amendment the federal government have to show 15th violations to be both recent and actual . Roberts stated clearly that congress are free to pass another voting rights act with precisely the same requirements as long as the areas listed are included due to recent violations not 50 year old violations that no longer occur .

Prohibition was a time in the United States when it was illegal to make, sell, or transport alcohol. It lasted from 1920 to 1933. There were many people and groups who supported Prohibition. Some of them believed that alcohol was a dangerous and unhealthy substance that caused problems like crime and family problems. They thought that if people couldn't buy alcohol, then there would be fewer of these problems. Other people supported Prohibition because they believed that it was against their religious beliefs to drink alcohol. They thought that making alcohol illegal would help people live better lives and be more moral. Prohibition was put into place by the 18th Amendment to the United States Constitution. This amendment made it illegal to make, sell, or transport alcohol throughout the country. However, Prohibition did not work very well. Many people continued to drink alcohol, and some even made it themselves or bought it from people who were selling it illegally. This led to a rise in crime, as people who wanted to make and sell alcohol often had to break the law in order to do so. Ultimately, Prohibition was repealed by the 21st Amendment to the United States Constitution, which made it legal to make, sell, and transport alcohol again.

As others have noted , translation is an extremely tricky business , and it 's pretty easy to introduce subtle changes in meaning ... but those differences can be important ( especially if you 're claiming that your book is The Actual , Infallible , Literal Word O ' God ) . For example , back in the 60s , US newspapers widely reported a story about Nikita Khrushchev shaking his fist and threatening the US by snarling , " we will bury you ! " But he did n't say that at all . What he said ( in Russian ) was closer to " we will be present at your funeral . " IOW , it was a boast : the mighty Soviet Union will be here long after decadent western capitalism has died . Not a threat . But that difference was used to drum up more war hysteria , and could have led the end of human life on Earth . In the case of the Bible , one word in particular has the potential to subtly change the meaning of a passage : thou . Today , that just sounds all Biblical and old - timey , but back when the KJV was produced , it had a more concrete meaning : it was the formal version of " you . " Many languages have formal and informal versions of " you , " which are used depending on whether you 're talking to a close friend or relative , or a stranger , your boss , or in some other formal setting . Thou was the English version of that , so if you translate a passage with " thou " to the modern English " you , " thou hast possibly introduced a subtle change in meaning . If the speaker is talking to somebody that they * should * use the formal thou , but say you instead , it carries the subtle connotation that the speaker is being rude or overly - forward .

Cold reading is a technique used by some people to make it seem like they have psychic abilities or are able to read people's minds. They do this by asking vague questions and making observations that could apply to a lot of people, and then gradually narrowing down their guesses based on the responses they get. For example, a cold reader might start off by saying something like "I sense that you have been through a lot of changes recently," which could apply to many people. If the person they are talking to nods or agrees, the cold reader might then say something more specific, like "I get the sense that you have recently moved to a new home or started a new job." If the person agrees again, the cold reader might then say something even more specific, like "I feel like you are having some difficulty adjusting to your new surroundings and are feeling a bit overwhelmed." Cold reading can be very effective because it is hard to prove that the person is not psychic, and people often want to believe that they have special abilities. It is important to remember, however, that cold reading is just a trick and that it is not a real psychic ability.

Fish swim in the direction they do because of the shape of their bodies and the way they move their fins. Fish are designed to be streamlined and move efficiently through water, and they swim upright in order to take advantage of this shape. If a fish were to swim upside down, its body would not be as streamlined and it would have more difficulty moving through the water. Fish also use their fins to swim, and they are most effective when the fins are used in a certain way. For example, a fish's tail fin, or caudal fin, is used to propel it forward through the water. When a fish moves its tail fin up and down, it creates a wave-like motion that pushes it through the water. If a fish were to swim upside down, its tail fin would not be able to create this wave-like motion as effectively, making it harder for the fish to swim. So, to sum it up, fish swim upright because it helps them move efficiently through the water and because their fins are most effective when used in a certain way.

Fevers are a common symptom of many types of illness, including infections and some types of inflammation. When the body has a fever, it means that the immune system is working to fight off an infection or other problem. One way the body tries to fight off the illness is by raising its internal temperature. This is why people with fevers often have a higher body temperature than normal. 
However, even though the body's temperature has increased, people with fevers may still feel cold. This is because the body's normal temperature control system is disrupted by the fever. The body's normal temperature control system uses a balance of heat production and heat loss to maintain a healthy body temperature. When the body has a fever, it may produce more heat than usual, but it may also lose more heat through the skin because the blood vessels in the skin dilate (expand) to try to lose heat. This can make people with fevers feel cold, even though their body temperature is higher than normal. 
It's also common for people with fevers to feel shivery or have chills. This is because the body is trying to warm itself up and generate more heat. The muscles may contract and relax rapidly (a process called shivering) to try to produce heat, and this can make the person feel cold.

Guys , guys , guys ... it 's not just brain size . You 're forgetting the whole ' bipedal ' thing . Humans walk on two legs instead of four . This means that the bone structure of our hips is vastly different than most other animals . One side effect of this , is that the birth canal is much narrower for us than for other animals , and it also puts constraints on the size & position of the uterus . As a result of these constraints , a human fetus is born when it is smaller than would be expected for closely - related animals of our size . Other animals ( like horses & cows & elephants ) get to gestate until they 're nearly developed enough to walk . A human fetus is pushed out literally as soon as it can breathe ( the hormone that initiates labor originates in the fetus ' lungs ) . There is a recent parenting trend of saying a newborn child is in the " fourth trimester"--this perspective has some biological foundation . In theory , humans could probably have evolved to be more self - sufficent ( i.e. ambulatory ) at birth despite being smaller . For example , Giant Pandas are stupid tiny when born ( 4oz--"the size of a stick of butter " ) and they 're crawling around in 2 - 3 months , although they nurse for 18 months . Our evolutionary path developed social structures , rather than physical changes to newborns , to ensure the survival of such small infants .

Once the rules and syntax for the language have been designed, a program called a compiler is used to translate the instructions written in the programming language into a form that the computer can understand and execute. The compiler converts the instructions into a series of ones and zeros, which are the basic building blocks that computers use to perform tasks.

So, to sum it up, a programming language is made by designing the rules and syntax for writing instructions, and then using a compiler to translate those instructions into a form that the computer can understand and execute. Does that make sense?

H.264 is a video compression format that is used to reduce the size of video files while maintaining good quality. It works by using algorithms to remove redundancies and inefficiencies in the data of a video file. This allows the file to be stored and transmitted more efficiently, without taking up as much space or requiring as much bandwidth.

H.264 is a big deal because it has become one of the most widely used video compression formats in the world. It is used to encode video for a variety of applications, including streaming video online, storing video on devices like DVDs and Blu-ray discs, and transmitting video over the internet or other networks. It is also used in a variety of industries, including entertainment, healthcare, education, and more.

In short, H.264 is important because it allows us to store and transmit video more efficiently, making it easier to watch and share video online.

It 's a school of thought that 's been somewhat formalized for a bit over thirty years . It basically started out on usenet , with alt.seduction.fast . The school of thought was pioneered Ross Jeffries , and it was mostly guys yammering about disingenuous tricks like using neurolinguistic programming ( NLP ) on drunk girls in bars to get them to come home with you . Others came out with new methods that used social ritual and premeditated interactions put together in " routines " to have similar effects without relying on the girl being drunk or open to NLP . " The Mystery Method " is one of the most popular ways of learning these routines , mostly designed and pushed by Erik Markovik AKA " Mystery " . IIRC , David Wygant and " Gunwitch " were also big names in " the community " around that time . As it gained in popularity , these authors began publicizing their works . Mostly , they started with forum subscriptions and " boot camps " ( where they would lead a small group of students through some coaching sessions then application in the real world , or " the field " ) . It became more commercialized , with real estate guru Eban Pagan ( AKA " David Deangelo " ) pushing a very popular series of motivational seminars , books , and such . The industry boomed in about 2007 when VH1 published a show featuring " Mystery " called " The Pickup Artist " . Popularity and popular hate of " the community " soared . The series pretty much crashed and burned - people seemed to just laugh at it . The second season in 2008 was much less popular , and a third season was not attempted . As " the community " has evolved since then , the focus has come to rest on self - improvement . The line of reasoning is that if you become an attractive , interesting , popular man , women will want to sleep with you . Most of the " routines " are targeted only to socially inept beginners who need a crutch to learn social conversation skills and gain confidence , and are generally unpopular except in that context . Most of the coaching is in self - improvement , encouragement to keep a wide social circle including platonic friendships with women , and proper exercise , hygiene , and clothing . Of course there are many claims of objectification of women ( and men ) , dominance games , and just plain being shallow ( chasing and befriending only attractive , popular people ) . Those are value judgments , not appropriate for this subreddit , and you 're best off locating and reading some of the material and coming to those conclusions on your own . To answer your question directly , about popular techniques now , it 's pretty much following a simplified outline of a social conversation with someone who will become a friend . Do that , and make it clear that you 're romantically or sexually interested , and you 'll tend to have success . To hijack the mystery method : 1 . Open - begin speaking to her . Either ask her a question relevant to her ( what she looks like , what she 's wearing ) or where you are ( something that just happened , something unique about the venue ) . Avoid complimenting her outright , buying her a drink , or anything else that appears to be presenting affection with anticipation of a return of affection . 1 . Comfort - discover and share commonalities , like drinks you like , vacation places you like , childhood memories , etc . Anything that evokes good memories and that you have in common is a good topic for conversation . 1 . Attraction - lead her through being attracted to you . Assume that she will be , allow her to demonstrate that she 's worth spending time talking to , and recognize her as a valueable person . 1 . Seduction - IF you actually are attracted to each other , begin touching her more ( part of the social acclimation that you should have learned as you practiced the other steps is basic social touch , like holding her arm or shoulder if you 're leading her somewhere , punctuating an exciting story by grabbing her hand , etc - now is when you push past that and put an arm around her or the like ) . If you 're both interested , the touching will proceed along a fairly well documented scale ( " escalation ladder " ) and you 'll move to a more private place to let what happens happen .

Graphs are made of nodes ( the dots or vertices ) and links ( the lines ) . Such a graph may have links without specified values , called an unweighted graph , or each link may have a value , aka a weighted graph . One weighted graph has nodes for each city and weighted links where the weight represents the distance between the cities . Traveling salesman problems use this sort of graph a lot . The links on a graph may be directional ( usually shown with an arrow ) , in what is called a directed graph , or they can be just lines , an undirected graph . Continuing the last example , if the weight is the cost to travel between cities ( perhaps in units of gasoline ) then you might have directed links if one city is up a steep hill from another . In that case the " going uphill " direction would have a larger weight ( cost ) than the " going downhill " direction .

Aliasing is a phenomenon that occurs when a signal is sampled at a rate that is not high enough to accurately represent the original signal. This can lead to errors in the sampled signal, which can be difficult to detect and correct.

An example of aliasing might be if you were trying to record a sound with a microphone, but the microphone was not able to capture all of the details of the sound. The recorded sound might be distorted or muffled, and it might not accurately represent the original sound.

The Nyquist frequency is a concept that is related to aliasing. It is the maximum frequency that can be accurately represented by a given sampling rate. If the sampling rate is too low, then frequencies above the Nyquist frequency will be aliased, or distorted, when they are sampled.

To explain this concept to a five-year-old, you might say that the Nyquist frequency is like a border that separates the frequencies that can be accurately recorded from the frequencies that can't. If a sound has a frequency that is higher than the Nyquist frequency, then it might not be recorded correctly, just like a person trying to cross a border might not be allowed to enter a country if they don't have the right paperwork.

Planes traveling east will experience a bit of a speed boost due to the Earth's rotation, while planes traveling west will experience a corresponding slowdown. Historically , pigeons carried messages only one way , to their home . They had to be transported manually before another flight . The German apothecary Julius Neubronner used carrier pigeons to deliver urgent medication.[4 ] In 1977 a similar carrier pigeon service was set up for the transport of laboratory specimens between two English hospitals . Every morning a basket with pigeons was taken from Plymouth General Hospital to Devonport Hospital . The birds then delivered unbreakable vials back to Plymouth as needed.[5 ] The 30 carrier pigeons became unnecessary in 1983 because of the closure of one of the hospitals.[6 ] In the 1980s a similar system existed between two French hospitals located in Granville and Avranche.[7 ]

There is a central unit which supplies many floors with chilled air . This unit is called an Air Handling Unit ( AHU ) . Before the Julian calendar was introduced in 46 BC, the Roman calendar had only ten months, with the year beginning in March. The months of September, October, November, and December were added to the end of the year later on, after the Julian calendar was adopted. The names of these months come from the Latin language and actually mean "seventh," "eighth," "ninth," and "tenth," respectively. Cancer is a disease in which abnormal cells in the body grow and divide in an uncontrolled way. When cancer cells grow in an organ, they can spread beyond that organ to other parts of the body. This is called metastasis.

If an organ is infected with cancer, removing it (a procedure called surgery) can often help to stop the cancer from spreading. However, it is not always possible to completely remove all of the cancer cells, especially if the cancer has already spread to other parts of the body.

Even if all of the cancer cells in an organ can be removed, it is not always possible to replace the infected organ with a donor organ. This is because the patient's body may reject the new organ, just like it can reject a transplanted organ in any other situation.

In addition, not all types of cancer can be treated with surgery. Some types of cancer, such as leukemia (a type of cancer that affects the blood and bone marrow), cannot be cured by removing an infected organ.

In summary, while removing an infected organ can sometimes be an effective way to treat cancer, it is not a cure-all. Other treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy, may be necessary to help kill any remaining cancer cells and prevent the cancer from coming back.

Fridges rely on a thermodynamic process known as a [ Heat Pump . ] ( URL_0 ) A quick overview of how they work however : 1 . Electricicty from you wall outlet runs a compressor pump . This compresses the refrigerant fluid , creating a hot fluid at high pressure . - Remember when you compress a fluid , it gets warm . 2 . This hot fluid at high pressure goes through a condensor ( often called the radiator ) on the back of the fridge . This allows the hot fluid to cool down , by losing it 's excess heat to the ambient air . In doing this , the fluid condenses into a liquid . 3 . This high pressure liquid then goes through an expander / throttle valve . This essentially drops the pressure of the fluid : oposite to at point 1 , a decompressing fluid cools down . Because the high pressure fluid was at ambient temperature , the expanding fluid ( past the valve ) will chill below ambient temperature . 4 . The sub - cooled fluid is then passed through an evaporator ( a long coil burried in the body of the fridge ) where it absorbs heat from it 's surroudnings and vaporises the remainder of the refrigerant fluid . Because it draws heat from within the box , the inside of the fridge gets cooler . This vapour then goes into the compressor again , and it starts all over again !

Understeer and oversteer are both terms that describe how a car handles when it is turning.

Understeer is when the front wheels of the car do not have enough grip, and the car does not turn as much as the driver wants it to. This can cause the car to go straight or even turn in the opposite direction of where the driver wants to go.

Oversteer is when the rear wheels of the car do not have enough grip, and the car turns more than the driver wants it to. This can cause the car to spin out or lose control.

Both understeer and oversteer can be dangerous and can cause accidents, so it is important for drivers to be aware of how their car is handling and make adjustments accordingly.

Sure! An internal combustion engine is a type of engine that runs on fuel, like gasoline or diesel. It's called an "internal combustion" engine because the fuel is burned inside the engine, rather than in a separate furnace like in a steam engine.

Here's how it works:

The engine has cylinders, which are tubes that move up and down.
Inside each cylinder is a piston, which is a metal rod that moves up and down.
The engine also has a spark plug, which is a little electrical device that makes a spark.
When you turn on the car, the spark plug sends a spark to the fuel in the cylinder, which makes it ignite and burn.
When the fuel burns, it creates hot gases that push the piston down.
The piston is connected to a crankshaft, which is a long rod that sticks out of the engine.
As the piston moves down, it turns the crankshaft.
The crankshaft is connected to the wheels of the car, so as it turns, it makes the car move.
After the piston pushes down, it moves back up again, ready for the next spark and burn.
That's the basic idea! I hope that helps explain how an internal combustion engine works. Let me know if you have any more questions.

There is no GED equivalent for a college degree because a college degree represents a higher level of education than a GED. The "half-life" of a substance is the amount of time it takes for half of the substance to decay, or break down, into something else. For example, if a substance has a half-life of 10 years, then after 10 years, half of the substance will have decayed, and after 20 years, half of the remaining half will have decayed, and so on. 
Measuring the "full" life of a substance would involve waiting until all of the substance has decayed, which could take a very long time. The half-life is a useful measure because it tells us how quickly the substance is decaying, and we can use this information to predict how much of the substance will remain after a certain amount of time. 
It's also worth noting that the half-life of a substance is always the same, no matter how much of the substance you have. This means that if you have a small amount of a substance with a long half-life, it will take a long time for it to decay, but if you have a large amount of the same substance, it will still take the same amount of time for half of it to decay.

The internet is like a neighborhood . Each connected device is its own house , and each house has different points of entry . You can think of things like your bank as being that guy with security cameras on every corner , double - thick steel doors , and trip mines on the walkway . Your home network is more like a pre - fab home tucked away on a quiet cul - de - sac . Internet security can be analogous to the entryways in a home . There are windows , doors , vents , etc . Sometimes information can be seen but not touched ( windows that do n’t open ) . Sometimes information can get in , but not out ( windows with a screen ) . Sometimes information can go both ways , so long as you have a specific login ( a door with a key ) . And sometimes * very specific * information can flow freely ( air vents , pipes , etc ) . That being said , hackers are like burglars . The average “ hack ” is just a random sniffer that walks around your house and checks out what windows and doors you have open . It might see a screen over an open window and think “ too hard ” , or it might find a door was left unlocked or open and think “ easy money ” . More complex , human hackers will try to find weaknesses in the House , such as a closed , but unlatched window , a crawl space with access to the home , etc . and some high - tech hacks can try to pick your lock , break your windows , or break down the door . The biggest threat to cyber security , as mentioned by someone else , is people . Sometimes simple mistakes are made , such as leaving the door open , a key under the mat , etc . Many hacks exploit the worries of others . They will call you and say your neighbor is in trouble , “ go help quick ! ” In your rush out the door you forget to lock it , and they rob you while you ’re away . Or they might disguise themselves as your nan and knock on the door . You open it only to find out that it was the Big Bad Wolf all along . Barring human error ( or stupidity ) , no network is truly secure . Anyone with enough time and money can break in , if you ’re worth it . A door can be opened , a window can be broken , a flaw in construction can be found and utilized , or someone can just straight up cut a whole in the wall . The real question is : “ are you worth it ? ” Remember your bank with the steel doors and the trip mines ? Well , an Oceans - Eleven level of hacking can still disable them and force / trick their way in , because that guy has gold inside . But you in your average home do n’t have anything much worth breaking in for , or at least it ca n’t be seen from a cursory glance through a window . As long as you keep your door locked , you ’re most likely fine .

For anyone thinking Schrodinger 's cat is an analogy for how small things work ... no , it 's not . It is a thought experiment designed to show that if things could operate the way quantum mechanics proposes at the scale of subatomic particles , there is nothing in principle that would stop that same effect from being made macroscopic . When Schrodinger proposed it , he meant it as a kind of " reductio ad absurdum " . He meant to say , " Look , if this is true at the small scale , then in principle , there 's no reason this cat ca n't be alive and dead at the same time ! " As it turns out , his reasoning was exactly spot on . Scientists have indeed made macroscopic objects exhibit quantum behavior . In principle , so far , all experiments indicate that if one were to literally carry out Schrodinger 's proposed thought experiment , we could in fact verify the superposition of states of both " live " and " dead " for a cat .

A planet is a large object in space that orbits, or goes around, the sun. The solar system, which is the group of objects that orbit the sun, includes eight planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

To be classified as a planet, an object must meet three criteria:

It must be in orbit around the sun. This means that it must be held in place by the sun's gravity and not be orbiting anything else.

It must be big enough to be round. This means that its own gravity pulls it into a shape that is mostly a sphere.

It must have cleared its orbit. This means that it has removed most of the other objects that might be in its path around the sun.

Pluto used to be considered a planet, but it was later reclassified as a "dwarf planet." This means that it meets the first two criteria for being a planet, but it does not meet the third criteria because it shares its orbit with other objects, such as the Kuiper Belt.

Geosynchronous orbits are special types of orbits that allow a satellite to stay in the same position above the Earth's surface. This is possible because the satellite is orbiting the Earth at the same speed that the Earth is spinning. Imagine you are standing on a playground merry-go-round. If you walk around the edge of the merry-go-round at the same speed that it is spinning, you will stay in the same place and not fall off. Similarly, a satellite in a geosynchronous orbit stays in the same place above the Earth because it is moving at the same speed as the Earth's rotation. Even though the satellite is not falling towards the Earth, it is still being pulled towards the Earth by the Earth's gravity. This is what keeps the satellite in orbit around the Earth. The satellite is constantly falling towards the Earth, but it is also constantly moving forward in its orbit, so it never actually hits the ground.

In the United States, having health insurance is important because it helps people pay for medical care when they get sick or injured. This helps to spread the cost of medical care more evenly among all the people in the country, rather than just those who are sick or injured.

Gaddafi was a left wing leader , and many well - meaning people in the rich countries supported him when he took power . This turned out to be against their better judgement , as it had with the Soviets before him - he took some very good ideas and gained power in a backwards country . He took the common path for left wing dictators in a right wing political climate , losing more and more of his original principles , retreating into conspiracy theories and oppression of his people , even abandoning the good things he had started off doing . When dictatorships of the left and right started to fall across Africa , the now fairly mad colonel refused to step down , regardless of what his people wanted . He accused the west of inciting violence in his peaceful regime and went into denial about the will his people had for change . He sent his remaining loyal troops to kill innocents ( " non - combatants " ) , starved the people of Misrata ( many to death ) and commanded that doctors should be killed if they treated rebel soldiers . Importantly , he is the only leader in the history of international justice who would be brought to trial with evidence of him personally ordering the atrocities .

Sure! A railgun is a type of gun that uses electricity to launch a projectile instead of gunpowder. It works by using two metal rails and a sliding armature to create a strong magnetic field. 
First, a very strong electrical current is passed through the rails. This creates a magnetic field that pushes against the armature, which is a conductive metal bar or rod that is mounted between the rails. When the magnetic field is strong enough, it can overcome the force of gravity and friction, causing the armature to slide along the rails at high speed. 
The armature is connected to the projectile, which is a small metal object that is meant to be launched from the gun. As the armature slides along the rails, it carries the projectile with it, propelling it out of the railgun and through the air. 
That's the basic idea, but there are a lot of technical details and engineering challenges involved in making a railgun work properly. Some railguns use additional components, such as a pulsed power supply or a plasma armature, to achieve even higher speeds and greater accuracy.

Antibacterial hand gels and soaps work by disrupting the cell walls or membranes of bacteria. They contain ingredients, such as alcohol or certain chemicals, that can break down the outer protective layer of bacteria and cause them to die. 
Our own cells, on the other hand, have different structures and functions compared to bacterial cells. Our cells have a stronger and more complex cell membrane that is more resistant to the ingredients in antibacterial hand gels and soaps. In addition, our cells also have other protective mechanisms in place that help them repair and defend against damage. 
It's important to note that while antibacterial hand gels and soaps can be effective at killing bacteria on our skin, they may not necessarily kill all types of bacteria. It's still important to wash your hands with soap and water to remove dirt and germs, and to follow good hand hygiene practices to prevent the spread of illness.
